title: Telephony & PSTN Fundamentals: The Legacy World VoIP Connects To
summary: How analog phones, the PSTN hierarchy, circuit switching, TDM carriers, ISDN, SS7 signaling, numbering plans, and PCM digitization work — the legacy telephony foundation that an embassy VoIP system must interwork with.
content:
An embassy's IP-based unified-communications platform never lives in isolation: it terminates onto the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) for calls to local government offices, host-country contacts, and emergency services. Understanding the legacy world is essential for designing gateways, dial plans, and survivable PSTN failover.

• Analog phones and the local loop: A traditional analog phone connects to the telephone company Central Office (CO) over a two-wire copper pair called the local loop. The CO supplies -48V DC. "Loop start" signaling means lifting the handset (going off-hook) closes the loop and draws current, which the CO detects to provide dial tone. Ringing voltage is roughly 90V AC at 20 Hz. Dial tone, busy tone, and ringback are call-progress tones; digits are sent as DTMF (dual-tone multi-frequency) tone pairs.

• PSTN hierarchy and circuit switching: The PSTN is a circuit-switched network — a dedicated 64 kbps path (a DS0) is reserved end-to-end for the duration of a call, guaranteeing constant bandwidth and low delay but wasting capacity during silence. Historically switches were arranged in a class hierarchy (Class 5 end offices/COs nearest subscribers, up through tandem and Class 4 toll switches). A Centrex service delivers PBX-like features from the CO instead of on-premises equipment; a key system is a small device giving each phone direct line buttons; a PBX is customer-owned switching on site.

• TDM and the digital carrier hierarchy: Voice is digitized and multiplexed using Time Division Multiplexing (TDM). A DS0 is one 64 kbps channel. In North America a T1/DS1 carries 24 DS0s (1.544 Mbps); a DS3 carries 28 T1s (~44.736 Mbps, 672 channels). The European E-carrier uses E1 = 32 timeslots at 2.048 Mbps (30 voice channels plus signaling and framing).

• ISDN: Integrated Services Digital Network delivers digital service over the local loop. BRI (Basic Rate) = 2B+D: two 64 kbps bearer (B) channels plus one 16 kbps data/signaling (D) channel. PRI (Primary Rate) = 23B+D in North America (on a T1) or 30B+D in Europe (on an E1); the D channel carries Q.931 call-control signaling. Embassy PBXs commonly connect to the PSTN over a PRI before (or alongside) SIP trunks.

• Signaling — CAS vs CCS and SS7: Channel Associated Signaling (CAS) embeds signaling within each voice channel (e.g., "robbed-bit" signaling on T1). Common Channel Signaling (CCS) uses a separate dedicated channel — the ISDN D channel, or, between carrier switches, Signaling System 7 (SS7). SS7 is the out-of-band network that sets up and tears down calls, performs number translation, and enables caller ID and toll-free routing. Awareness level only: officers should know SS7 is the carrier signaling backbone and a historic security/spoofing concern.

• Numbering and caller ID: The North American Numbering Plan (NANP) uses the format +1-NPA-NXX-XXXX (area code, exchange, subscriber). Internationally, E.164 numbers begin with a country code (e.g., +1 North America, +44 UK, +49 Germany, +34 Spain). Caller Line Identification (CLI / Caller ID) is delivered to analog phones via FSK modem tones between the first and second ring bursts.

• Analog-to-digital conversion (PCM): To carry analog voice digitally, the signal is sampled and quantized using Pulse Code Modulation. By the Nyquist theorem, sampling must be at least twice the highest frequency; telephone voice is band-limited to ~3.4 kHz, so the standard sampling rate is 8,000 samples/second (8 kHz). Each sample is encoded in 8 bits, giving 8000 × 8 = 64,000 bps = 64 kbps — exactly one DS0 and the bitrate of the G.711 codec. Companding compresses the dynamic range non-linearly so 8 bits sound like more: North America/Japan use mu-law (μ-law), the rest of the world uses A-law. A gateway bridging regions must transcode between them.
keyPoints: Loop start: off-hook closes the loop drawing CO current; ring is ~90V AC 20 Hz, talk battery is -48V DC
PSTN is circuit-switched — a dedicated 64 kbps DS0 path is held for the whole call
CO/Class 5 end office serves subscribers; Centrex = CO-hosted PBX features, key system = small line-button device, PBX = on-site switch
DS0 = 64 kbps; T1/DS1 = 24 DS0 = 1.544 Mbps; E1 = 30 voice + 2 = 2.048 Mbps; DS3 = 28 T1
ISDN BRI = 2B+D (2×64k + 16k); PRI = 23B+D (T1) or 30B+D (E1); D channel carries Q.931
CAS embeds signaling in the voice channel; CCS uses a separate channel (D channel / SS7)
SS7 is the out-of-band carrier signaling network for call setup, number translation, and caller ID
E.164 international numbering uses country codes; NANP format is +1-NPA-NXX-XXXX
PCM: 8 kHz sampling × 8 bits = 64 kbps = G.711 = one DS0; Nyquist requires ≥2× the 3.4 kHz voice band
Companding: mu-law (North America/Japan) vs A-law (rest of world); cross-region gateways must transcode

flashcards.front: What is the local loop?
flashcards.back: The two-wire copper pair connecting a subscriber's analog phone to the telephone company Central Office (CO).

flashcards.front: In loop start signaling, what does going off-hook do?
flashcards.back: It closes the loop and draws DC current from the CO, which the CO detects and responds to with dial tone.

flashcards.front: What voltage and frequency is standard ringing on an analog line?
flashcards.back: About 90V AC at 20 Hz (talk battery is -48V DC).

flashcards.front: What does it mean that the PSTN is circuit-switched?
flashcards.back: A dedicated 64 kbps path (DS0) is reserved end-to-end for the entire call duration, giving constant bandwidth and low delay.

flashcards.front: Difference between a PBX, Centrex, and a key system?
flashcards.back: PBX = customer-owned on-site switch; Centrex = PBX-like features delivered from the carrier's CO; key system = small device giving phones direct line buttons.

flashcards.front: How many DS0s are in a T1/DS1 and what is its rate?
flashcards.back: 24 DS0 channels at 1.544 Mbps.

flashcards.front: How does an E1 differ from a T1?
flashcards.back: E1 = 2.048 Mbps with 32 timeslots (30 voice + 1 framing + 1 signaling); T1 = 1.544 Mbps with 24 channels.

flashcards.front: What are the channel structures of ISDN BRI and PRI?
flashcards.back: BRI = 2B+D (two 64k bearer + one 16k data). PRI = 23B+D in North America (T1) or 30B+D in Europe (E1).

flashcards.front: What does the ISDN D channel carry?
flashcards.back: Out-of-band call-control signaling using Q.931 (Common Channel Signaling).

flashcards.front: CAS vs CCS?
flashcards.back: Channel Associated Signaling embeds signaling within each voice channel; Common Channel Signaling uses a separate dedicated channel (D channel or SS7).

flashcards.front: What is SS7 and why does it matter at awareness level?
flashcards.back: Signaling System 7 — the out-of-band carrier signaling network for call setup, number translation, and caller ID; historically a source of spoofing/security concern.

flashcards.front: How is 64 kbps derived for a voice channel (PCM)?
flashcards.back: 8 kHz sampling × 8 bits per sample = 64,000 bps = 64 kbps (one DS0, the G.711 bitrate).

flashcards.front: Why 8 kHz sampling for telephone voice?
flashcards.back: Nyquist: sample at ≥2× the highest frequency; voice is band-limited to ~3.4 kHz, so 8 kHz suffices.

flashcards.front: Mu-law vs A-law companding — who uses which?
flashcards.back: Mu-law (μ-law) in North America and Japan; A-law in the rest of the world. Gateways between regions must transcode.

flashcards.front: What is the E.164 / NANP number format?
flashcards.back: E.164 = international numbers led by a country code (+1, +44, +34...). NANP format: +1-NPA-NXX-XXXX (area, exchange, subscriber).

flashcards.front: How is analog Caller ID delivered to a phone?
flashcards.back: As FSK modem tones sent between the first and second ring bursts.

title: Unified Communications & Collaboration in the Embassy
summary: How UC&C unifies voice, video, chat, presence, and conferencing — covering presence/IM (XMPP/SIMPLE), softphones and mobility, video conferencing (H.323 vs SIP, codecs, MCU/SVC), WebRTC, contact centers, federation, unified messaging, and CTI.
content:
Unified Communications & Collaboration (UC&C) is the convergence of all real-time and near-real-time communication modalities — voice telephony, video, instant messaging, presence, conferencing, and content sharing — onto a single integrated platform with a common directory and consistent user experience. For an embassy this means one identity reaches a diplomat whether they are at their desk phone, laptop softphone, or mobile device, across posts worldwide.

• Presence and instant messaging: Presence is real-time availability status (available, busy, in a meeting, offline) that lets colleagues choose the right channel before interrupting. The two main standards are XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol, the open Jabber standard) and SIMPLE (SIP for Instant Messaging and Presence Leveraging Extensions), which extends SIP using SUBSCRIBE/NOTIFY for presence and MESSAGE for IM. Presence is the connective tissue of UC: it drives "click to call" and routing decisions.

• Softphones, mobility, and hot-desking: A softphone is a software client (on a PC, tablet, or smartphone) that registers to the call server like a hardware phone. Extension mobility lets a user log into any physical desk phone and have it adopt their extension, speed dials, and profile — useful for shared or rotating embassy workspaces. Hot-desking is the same idea applied to unassigned desks. BYOD and mobile clients extend the single business identity (one number, one voicemail) to personal devices over Wi-Fi or cellular data.

• Video conferencing — H.323 vs SIP: Two signaling families set up multimedia sessions. H.323 is the older ITU umbrella standard (with gatekeepers, H.245, etc.) still common in legacy video endpoints; SIP is the modern IETF standard now dominant and used by most new UC platforms. Video media uses codecs such as H.264/AVC (and H.265/HEVC) and the royalty-free VP8/VP9; audio often uses Opus. Multipoint conferences are mixed either by a centralized MCU (Multipoint Control Unit) that decodes and re-mixes all streams, or via SVC (Scalable Video Coding), where endpoints send layered streams and a lighter selective-forwarding unit forwards appropriate layers per participant.

• WebRTC: Web Real-Time Communication brings voice, video, and data directly into the browser with no plugins, using JavaScript APIs (getUserMedia, RTCPeerConnection). It mandates encryption (SRTP/DTLS), uses ICE/STUN/TURN for NAT traversal, and the audio codec Opus and video VP8/H.264. WebRTC powers click-to-call widgets, guest video meetings, and citizen-services portals where an external user joins without installing software.

• Content sharing and conferencing: Beyond audio/video, UC platforms provide screen and application/document sharing, virtual whiteboards, and persistent team chat rooms, turning a call into a collaborative session — important for reviewing documents across distributed embassy teams.

• Contact center / ACD: A contact center adds an Automatic Call Distributor (ACD) that queues inbound contacts and routes them to agents by skill, availability, and priority, with supervisor dashboards and reporting. An embassy consular line or visa enquiry desk is effectively a small contact center using ACD queues and an IVR front end.

• Federation and unified messaging: Federation lets two separate organizations' UC systems exchange presence, IM, and calls securely across a trust boundary (e.g., an embassy federating with its home foreign ministry or with an allied mission), typically over SIP/XMPP with TLS between edge/SBC servers. Unified messaging consolidates voicemail, email, and fax into one inbox — voicemail-to-email delivers a recorded message (often with speech-to-text transcription) as an email attachment, so a diplomat traveling can retrieve voicemail from their mail client. CTI (Computer Telephony Integration) links the phone system to applications — screen-pop of caller records, click-to-call from a directory or CRM — tying communications to workflows.
keyPoints: UC&C unifies voice, video, IM, presence, conferencing, and content sharing on one platform with a single identity/directory
Presence = real-time availability status; standards are XMPP (open Jabber) and SIMPLE (SIP SUBSCRIBE/NOTIFY/MESSAGE)
A softphone is a software endpoint that registers like a hardware phone; extension mobility/hot-desking move a user's profile to any device/desk
H.323 = older ITU video signaling (gatekeepers); SIP = modern dominant IETF standard
Video codecs: H.264/H.265 and royalty-free VP8/VP9; conference audio often Opus
MCU centrally decodes and re-mixes all streams; SVC sends layered streams forwarded selectively per participant
WebRTC = plugin-free browser RTC; mandatory encryption (DTLS/SRTP), ICE/STUN/TURN, Opus + VP8/H.264
ACD (Automatic Call Distributor) queues and skills-routes contact-center calls; consular/visa lines are small contact centers
Federation securely exchanges presence/IM/calls between separate UC organizations over TLS via edge/SBC
Unified messaging merges voicemail/email/fax; voicemail-to-email delivers (often transcribed) messages to the inbox; CTI enables screen-pop and click-to-call

flashcards.front: What is Unified Communications & Collaboration (UC&C)?
flashcards.back: The convergence of voice, video, instant messaging, presence, conferencing, and content sharing onto one integrated platform with a common identity and directory.

flashcards.front: What is presence in UC?
flashcards.back: Real-time availability status (available, busy, away, offline) that helps colleagues pick the right channel and drives routing/click-to-call.

flashcards.front: Name the two main presence/IM standards.
flashcards.back: XMPP (open Jabber standard) and SIMPLE (SIP for Instant Messaging and Presence — uses SUBSCRIBE/NOTIFY and MESSAGE).

flashcards.front: What is a softphone?
flashcards.back: A software client on a PC or mobile device that registers to the call server and behaves like a hardware phone.

flashcards.front: Extension mobility vs hot-desking?
flashcards.back: Extension mobility lets a user log into any desk phone and load their extension/profile; hot-desking applies the same to unassigned shared desks.

flashcards.front: H.323 vs SIP for video?
flashcards.back: H.323 is the older ITU signaling standard (gatekeepers, H.245); SIP is the modern IETF standard now dominant in UC.

flashcards.front: Common video and conference audio codecs?
flashcards.back: Video: H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC, and royalty-free VP8/VP9. Conference audio: commonly Opus.

flashcards.front: MCU vs SVC for multiparty video?
flashcards.back: MCU centrally decodes and re-mixes all streams into one; SVC sends layered/scalable streams that a forwarding unit selectively relays per participant.

flashcards.front: What is WebRTC?
flashcards.back: Browser-native real-time voice/video/data with no plugins, using getUserMedia/RTCPeerConnection, mandatory DTLS/SRTP encryption, ICE/STUN/TURN, and Opus/VP8/H.264.

flashcards.front: What does an ACD do?
flashcards.back: An Automatic Call Distributor queues inbound contacts and routes them to agents by skill, availability, and priority — the core of a contact center.

flashcards.front: What is UC federation?
flashcards.back: A secure trust relationship letting two separate organizations' UC systems exchange presence, IM, and calls (e.g., over SIP/XMPP with TLS via edge/SBC servers).

flashcards.front: What is voicemail-to-email / unified messaging?
flashcards.back: Consolidating voicemail, email, and fax into one inbox; voicemail is delivered (often with transcription) as an email attachment.

flashcards.front: What is CTI?
flashcards.back: Computer Telephony Integration — linking the phone system to applications for screen-pop of caller data and click-to-call from a directory or CRM.

flashcards.front: Why is presence called the connective tissue of UC?
flashcards.back: It signals availability that drives routing decisions, click-to-call, and choosing the right modality before interrupting someone.

flashcards.front: Give an embassy example of a contact center.
flashcards.back: A consular or visa enquiry line using ACD queues with an IVR front end to route callers to the right officers.

title: VoIP Security, Resilience & Operations for the Mission
summary: VoIP-specific threats (toll fraud, eavesdropping, SPIT, registration hijacking, RTP flooding/DoS, caller-ID spoofing) and the defenses, survivability design, capacity planning, troubleshooting tools, and nomadic E911 needed to run a secure, always-available embassy voice system.
content:
A converged voice system is a critical embassy service and an attractive target: voice carries sensitive diplomatic conversation, and a compromised system can leak intelligence, incur fraud charges, or go silent during a crisis. Security, resilience, and disciplined operations must be designed in, not bolted on.

• VoIP-specific threats: Toll fraud — attackers exploit a compromised PBX or weak SIP credentials to place expensive international/premium-rate calls. Eavesdropping/wiretapping — unencrypted RTP can be captured and replayed as audio, a direct confidentiality breach for diplomatic calls. SPIT (Spam over Internet Telephony) and vishing (voice phishing) — automated or social-engineering voice attacks. SIP registration hijacking — an attacker registers as a legitimate extension to intercept or place calls. DoS / RTP flooding — overwhelming call servers or media paths to deny service. Caller-ID spoofing — forging the calling number to impersonate a trusted party (e.g., posing as the front office).

• Defenses: Encrypt both signaling and media — TLS for SIP and SRTP for RTP — so calls cannot be read or hijacked in transit. Deploy a Session Border Controller (SBC) at the network edge for topology hiding (concealing internal addressing), call admission control, and protocol normalization. Enforce strong SIP authentication (digest with strong, unique credentials; certificate-based where possible) and disable default/unused accounts. Segment voice onto a dedicated voice VLAN, separated from data, with ACLs and firewall rules restricting which hosts can reach the call servers. Be cautious with SIP-ALG on firewalls — poorly implemented ALGs mangle SIP and break calls; an SBC is usually preferable. Run fraud monitoring with alerting on unusual call patterns (off-hours, high-cost destinations, call-volume spikes) and set per-class international dialing restrictions.

• Survivability: Voice must keep working when the WAN, a data center, or power fails. SRST (Survivable Remote Site Telephony) / local survivability lets a branch or remote post's phones fall back to a local gateway and continue making PSTN calls if the link to the central call server is lost. Deploy redundant/clustered call servers (active/standby or geographically distributed) so no single failure silences the post. Maintain PSTN failover so calls reroute over PRI or a backup SIP trunk if the primary path drops. Plan power: IP phones draw Power over Ethernet (PoE) from switches, so the switches and call servers must be on UPS (and ideally generator) backup — otherwise a power cut kills phones that legacy analog lines would have survived. Geographic redundancy spreads call control across sites for disaster resilience.

• Capacity and bandwidth planning: Each call's bandwidth = codec payload plus IP/UDP/RTP and layer-2 overhead. A G.711 call is roughly 80–90 kbps each way on Ethernet; G.729 is roughly 25–30 kbps each way. Multiply by expected concurrent calls, add headroom, and use Call Admission Control to refuse new calls rather than degrade existing ones once the budget is reached. Size the WAN/SIP trunk for busy-hour concurrency, not total users.

• Troubleshooting tools: Use packet capture (Wireshark, which can decode SIP ladder diagrams and even play back captured RTP) to diagnose signaling and media problems. Monitor RTCP reports and MOS (Mean Opinion Score) estimates to detect jitter, packet loss, and latency degrading call quality. Check CDRs for routing and fraud, and use SBC/call-server logs for registration and authentication failures.

• 911 routing and nomadic E911: Because an IP phone or softphone can physically move while keeping the same number, the system cannot assume location from the number. Nomadic E911 requires associating each device with its current civic location (so emergency calls route to the correct Public Safety Answering Point with an accurate address) and prompting roaming/softphone users to confirm location. In the embassy context this maps to ensuring local host-country emergency numbers and on-site security are reachable and that a moved or teleworking handset still routes emergencies correctly.
keyPoints: Top VoIP threats: toll fraud, eavesdropping on unencrypted RTP, SPIT/vishing, SIP registration hijacking, DoS/RTP flooding, caller-ID spoofing
Core defenses: TLS for signaling + SRTP for media encrypt and protect calls end to end
An SBC at the edge provides topology hiding, call admission control, and protocol normalization
Use strong/unique SIP digest credentials, disable defaults, and put voice on a segmented VLAN with ACLs/firewalls
Be wary of SIP-ALG on firewalls — buggy ALGs break SIP; prefer an SBC
Fraud monitoring + international dialing restrictions catch and limit toll-fraud abuse
SRST/local survivability keeps remote-site phones calling the PSTN when the central call server is unreachable
Resilience needs redundant/geographically distributed call servers, PSTN failover, and UPS/generator backing PoE switches
Bandwidth per call: ~80–90 kbps for G.711, ~25–30 kbps for G.729 (incl. overhead); size trunks for busy-hour concurrency and use CAC
Troubleshoot with packet capture (Wireshark SIP/RTP), RTCP/MOS analysis, CDRs, and SBC logs; nomadic E911 must map moving devices to current civic location

flashcards.front: What is VoIP toll fraud?
flashcards.back: Attackers exploiting a compromised PBX or weak SIP credentials to place expensive international or premium-rate calls at the victim's expense.

flashcards.front: Why is unencrypted RTP a confidentiality risk?
flashcards.back: Captured RTP packets can be reassembled and played back as audio, exposing the conversation — serious for diplomatic calls.

flashcards.front: What is SIP registration hijacking?
flashcards.back: An attacker registers as a legitimate extension to intercept incoming calls or place calls as that user.

flashcards.front: What are SPIT and vishing?
flashcards.back: SPIT = Spam over Internet Telephony (bulk unwanted calls); vishing = voice phishing using social engineering over the phone.

flashcards.front: Which two encryption protocols protect VoIP signaling and media?
flashcards.back: TLS encrypts SIP signaling; SRTP encrypts the RTP media.

flashcards.front: What three security functions does an SBC provide at the edge?
flashcards.back: Topology hiding (concealing internal addressing), call admission control, and SIP protocol normalization/repair.

flashcards.front: Why be cautious with SIP-ALG on firewalls?
flashcards.back: Poorly implemented SIP Application Layer Gateways mangle SIP messages and break calls; an SBC is usually the better choice.

flashcards.front: What is SRST / local survivability?
flashcards.back: Survivable Remote Site Telephony — remote phones fall back to a local gateway to keep making PSTN calls when the central call server is unreachable.

flashcards.front: Why must PoE switches and call servers be on UPS?
flashcards.back: IP phones draw power from the switch over PoE, so a power outage kills phones that legacy analog lines would have survived unless backed by UPS/generator.

flashcards.front: Approximate bandwidth of a G.711 vs G.729 call?
flashcards.back: G.711 ≈ 80–90 kbps each way; G.729 ≈ 25–30 kbps each way (including IP/UDP/RTP and layer-2 overhead).

flashcards.front: Why size VoIP trunks for busy-hour concurrency rather than total users?
flashcards.back: Only a fraction of users call simultaneously; bandwidth/CAC must cover peak concurrent calls, not the full user count.

flashcards.front: What does Call Admission Control do under load?
flashcards.back: Refuses new calls once the bandwidth budget is reached, protecting the quality of calls already in progress.

flashcards.front: Which tool decodes SIP ladder diagrams and can replay captured RTP?
flashcards.back: Wireshark (packet capture).

flashcards.front: What do RTCP and MOS tell an operator?
flashcards.back: RTCP reports and the Mean Opinion Score estimate call quality, revealing jitter, packet loss, and latency degradation.

flashcards.front: What problem does nomadic E911 solve?
flashcards.back: A mobile IP phone/softphone keeps its number while moving, so location can't be inferred from the number; nomadic E911 ties the device to its current civic address to route emergencies to the right PSAP.

flashcards.front: How does caller-ID spoofing threaten an embassy?
flashcards.back: An attacker forges the calling number to impersonate a trusted party (e.g., the front office) for social engineering or fraud.
